front: List the six phases of the SDLC loop in seller-friendly order (Plan → … → Monitor).
back: Plan, Code, Review, Test, Deploy, Monitor—delivery as a loop (where time is lost, failures are expensive, governance applies).

front: What do engineering buyers actually argue about regarding the SDLC—not the textbook theory?
back: Where time is lost, where failures are expensive, and what must be governed (security, compliance, reliability).

front: What does an IC engineer optimize for—and what do they fear in a new tool?
back: Speed, focus, fewer context switches; fears friction and tools that feel like “bossware.”

front: What does a tech lead or staff engineer optimize for—and what do they fear about automation?
back: Architecture risk, maintainability, review burden; fears low-quality or unsafe automation.

front: What does an engineering manager optimize for? A director or VP of engineering?
back: EM: team throughput, hiring/retention, predictability, less thrash. Director/VP: delivery commitments, org scale, engineering brand, outages, inefficiency.

front: In many enterprise deals, who is the economic buyer at the CTO/VP level—and what does Security/IT optimize for?
back: CTO/VP Eng often owns platform bets, security posture, vendor sprawl, leverage. Security/IT: data handling, access control, audit evidence, procurement alignment.

front: Name four surfaces in the typical developer “collaboration stack.”
back: IDE; Git + PRs; CI/CD; chat/tickets (e.g. Slack/Teams + Jira/Linear)—coordination across where work happens.

front: In one sentence: what are you really selling when you sell Cursor—not “a chat window”?
back: Less context switching across those surfaces when work spans multiple files and deep repo context.

front: Name three recurring frictions AI coding assistants are built to reduce (from the module).
back: Context loading cost, review latency, boilerplate/repetitive edits across services, knowledge silos (onboarding, legacy, undocumented domains)—examples, not an exhaustive list.

front: Give one strong discovery question from the module that reveals workflow—not trivia.
back: For example: “Walk me through the last change that touched more than three files—where did the team lose time?” (Also: who owns review standards for AI-generated changes; where engineers look first when prod breaks.)

front: In strategic accounts, multiple personas co-own the decision. What is your job regarding proof?
back: Sequence proof so each stakeholder hears what maps to their KPIs—without sounding like a different script in every room.

front: Why does coming from cyber-resilience sales not automatically fill the gap for dev-tool selling?
back: Risk/economic framing helps, but you still need how engineering work flows and how technical buyers evaluate tools that touch the SDLC.

front: What does the Plan phase actually produce in a healthy team?
back: A sliced backlog of changes the team understands and is ready to work on—not a giant doc nobody reads.

front: What is happening in the Code phase—not the slogan, the reality?
back: Engineers implementing changes in an IDE with tight feedback loops (lint, type-check, local tests).

front: What does the Review phase produce besides an approval click?
back: Risk catch, knowledge spread across the team, and a record of why the change is shaped the way it is.

front: Name three kinds of validation in the Test phase.
back: Unit tests, integration tests, and QA/canary checks—proving the change works before broad rollout.

front: Two mechanisms that make the Deploy phase safer in modern teams?
back: Progressive rollout (e.g. canaries) and feature flags—shipping to users in steps you can revert.

front: Why is Monitor the phase that should reshape Plan?
back: Production signals (incidents, latency, adoption) are the inputs that prioritize the next planning cycle.

front: Director vs VP Engineering—what shifts as you go up?
back: Director focuses on delivery commitments and team scaling; VP carries platform decisions, vendor consolidation, and engineering brand at scale.

front: What two acronyms most often signal Security/IT has joined the conversation?
back: SSO (often via SAML or OIDC) and SCIM—identity and lifecycle management.

front: Why is “context loading cost” a credible friction to name on a discovery call?
back: Engineers spend real time figuring out where a change actually lives in a large repo before they can write code.

front: Why is review latency a meaningful friction even when individual PRs are small?
back: Small PRs accumulate wait time across many engineers; large PRs trade waiting for reviewer cognitive load.

front: How do you defuse the IC engineer’s “bossware” fear when introducing a new AI tool?
back: Lead with autonomy, flow, and opt-in usage—not surveillance metrics or coercive rollout.

front: Give one strong question to surface whether AI-generated change has any governance owner.
back: “Who owns review standards for AI-generated changes today, and how is that being enforced?”

front: In one sentence, the Cursor pitch—not the buzzword version.
back: Less context switching across IDE, PRs, CI, chat, and tickets, especially on multi-file work that needs deep repo context.
